coredata.dc:title: Reactive carbon dots/polysiloxane composites cross-linked silicone resin adhesives for stable white LED constructing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Liya
Wang, Wei
Jin, Peng
Sun, Zhengguang
Zhan, Yuan
Jiang, Bingbing
coredata.dc:description: Current high-performance commercial white LEDs typically rely on Ce:Y3Al5O12 (Ce:YAG) phosphors and their dispersed encapsulating material. However, Ce:YAG often has large particle sizes and does not react with the encapsulating material, causing stratification and sedimentation. To address this problem, a novel reactive carbon dots/polysiloxane (R-CDs/PS) composite was synthesized through a one-step solvothermal process involving citric acid, urea, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), and vinyltrimethoxysilane (VTMS). The composite exhibits yellow emission at 528 nm under UV excitation and has a particle size of approximately 150 nm. To improve integration, vinyl adhesive (vinyl AD) and vinyl MDQ resin were designed and synthesized to enhance adhesion and mechanical strength. When dispersed in silicone resin adhesives (SRAs), R-CDs/PS composite forms a stable color conversion layer, avoiding stratification due to its nanoscale size and chemical cross-linking with SRAs. White LEDs can be easily produced by casting R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs onto LED chips, followed by high-temperature curing. These LEDs exhibit desirable optical properties, including CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.34), a CRI of 82.2, and a CCT of 5355 K, making them suitable for indoor lighting. This study not only introduces a practical approach for fabricating fluorescent and encapsulating materials but also offers a valuable strategy for improving the stability of white LEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots/polysiloxane composites
Cross-linked
Silicone resin adhesives
White LEDs

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of yellow and white LEDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Our work is financially supported by the Foundation of Three Gorges Laboratory (SK232014)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the composition and the architecture of the white LEDs, which consist of R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of R-CDs/PS composites (insets: photo under visible light (left), and under 365 nm UV (right)). (b) PL emission spectra of R-CDs/PS composites excited from 360 nm to 495 nm.
(a) FESEM and (b) HRTEM images of R-CDs/PS composites.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of R-CDs/PS composite. (b, c, d) High resolution XPS spectra of R-CDs/PS composites for N 1s, C 1s, and Si 2p, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of (a) vinyl AD and (b) vinyl MDQ resin.
(a) Tensile strength-elongation curves and (b) stress-strain curves of SRAs with different vinyl MDQ resin contents.

(a) Shear strength curve of SRAs with varying vinyl AD content. (b) Red ink penetration experiments for SRAs with and without vinyl AD (1, 2). (c) Shear strength curves of SRAs on Ag-PPA (silver-polyphthalamide), Ag-Ag (silver-silver), and Al-Al (aluminum-aluminum) substrates with different R-CDs/PS composites contents. (d) Corresponding photographs of bond failure interfaces (inset: 1–3). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) photographs of the fluorescent layer formed by cured SRAs with different fractions of R-CDs/PS composites under visible light (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). (b) UV–Vis transmission, (c) UV–Vis absorption, (d) PL intensity, and (e) FT-IR spectra of these fluorescent layers.

(a, b) The emission spectra and images of LEDs using R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs at ratios of 7:100 and 10:100, respectively. (c) The CIE coordinates of LEDs with varying R-CDs/PS composite contents. (d) CIE of R-CDs/PS composite in SRAs before and after curing. (e) The emission spectra of R-CDs/PS composites under operating currents ranging from 20 to 100 mA. (f) The emission spectra of white LEDs over different operating time intervals.

head.ce:title: Reactive carbon dots/polysiloxane composites cross-linked silicone resin adhesives for stable white LED constructing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, 430062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan, 430062, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Current high-performance commercial white LEDs typically rely on Ce:Y3Al5O12 (Ce:YAG) phosphors and their dispersed encapsulating material. However, Ce:YAG often has large particle sizes and does not react with the encapsulating material, causing stratification and sedimentation. To address this problem, a novel reactive carbon dots/polysiloxane (R-CDs/PS) composite was synthesized through a one-step solvothermal process involving citric acid, urea, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), and vinyltrimethoxysilane (VTMS). The composite exhibits yellow emission at 528 nm under UV excitation and has a particle size of approximately 150 nm. To improve integration, vinyl adhesive (vinyl AD) and vinyl MDQ resin were designed and synthesized to enhance adhesion and mechanical strength. When dispersed in silicone resin adhesives (SRAs), R-CDs/PS composite forms a stable color conversion layer, avoiding stratification due to its nanoscale size and chemical cross-linking with SRAs. White LEDs can be easily produced by casting R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs onto LED chips, followed by high-temperature curing. These LEDs exhibit desirable optical properties, including CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.34), a CRI of 82.2, and a CCT of 5355 K, making them suitable for indoor lighting. This study not only introduces a practical approach for fabricating fluorescent and encapsulating materials but also offers a valuable strategy for improving the stability of white LEDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent materials play a vital role in numerous applications, especially in white light-emitting diodes (LEDs), which offer several advantages over traditional lighting sources [1,2]. These advantages include compact size, safety, long lifespan, high integration, environmental friendliness, and high reliability [3–5]. As a result, white LEDs have become the preferred lighting solution in various fields, such as daily lighting, aerospace, electrical engineering, and transportation [6–8]. The construction of LEDs typically involves dispersing luminescent materials within an encapsulating material, which is then deposited onto a suitable chip [9]. The stability and dispersion of the luminescent materials within the encapsulating material, as well as the adhesion between the encapsulating material and the chip, are critical factors that directly impact the performance of LEDs, affecting brightness, lifespan, and applicability [10]. Currently, commercial white LEDs are predominantly produced by combining blue LED chips with yellow phosphors (Ce:YAG) [11,12]. However, Ce:YAG typically has a relatively large particle size and does not actively interact with the encapsulating material, leading to issues such as stratification and sedimentation, which can negatively impact light efficiency and stability. Moreover, Ce:YAG is derived from non-renewable resources and poses environmental and health risks [13]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop novel, non-toxic luminescent materials that can actively interact with the encapsulating material. Such materials would help mitigate issues related to delamination and sedimentation while simultaneously enhancing light stability.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained considerable attention for white LED applications due to their low cost, eco-friendliness, renewability, and ease of preparation [14–16]. Their fluorescence properties can be tuned across a wide spectrum, making them ideal for producing vibrant multicolored LEDs [17]. However, CDs often suffer from aggregation-induced quenching, which lowers their concentration within the encapsulating material and reduces the overall light output intensity of the LED system [18–21]. To overcome this limitation, composite materials incorporating CDs, such as NaCl crystals, starch particles, and polymers, have been investigated [22–24]. Among these, CDs doped with polysiloxane composites have garnered significant interest as color-converting phosphors for LEDs. Polysiloxane not only has stable chemical properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility, but it can also be adjusted to optimize wear resistance, mechanical properties, and light transmittance, enabling various morphologies and properties in CDs/polysiloxane composites. The flexible spatial arrangement and tunable molecular weight of polysiloxane allow precise control over the inter-CDs distances, creating a spatial hindrance effect that enhances the solid-state fluorescence of the CDs/polysiloxane composites [25–28]. However, research on the dispersion and stability of CDs/polysiloxane composites within encapsulating materials remains limited.

Common encapsulating materials for LEDs include organic polymers like polyvinyl alcohol, polyvinylpyrrolidone, and silicone-based materials [29,30]. Among these, silicone materials are preferred due to their superior properties, such as high-temperature resistance, transparency, vulcanization resistance, water resistance, UV resistance, and low shrinkage [31]. Silicone resin is produced through the hydrolysis and condensation of silane monomers, facilitated by acidic or basic catalysts [32]. By adjusting the proportions of the initial substances, various molecular structures, such as trapezoidal, branched, and cage-shaped forms, can be designed to enhance material properties, including reinforcement, toughness, and adhesion [33].

In this study, yellow-emitting carbon dots/polysiloxane (R-CDs/PS) composites with reactive vinyl groups were synthesized using a one-step solvothermal technique. Vinyl AD and vinyl MDQ resins were also prepared as encapsulating materials to enhance adhesion and mechanical strength. SRAs exhibit excellent mechanical properties, adhesive strength, and high heat resistance. Additionally, R-CDs/PS composites dispersed within SRAs form a stable color conversion layer, free of stratification and sedimentation, due to chemical cross-linking between R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs. The resulting white LEDs have a CIE of (0.33, 0.34), a CRI of 82.2, and a CCT of 5355 K, making them ideal for indoor lighting. This research introduces a practical strategy for developing novel fluorescent materials and encapsulants for white LEDs, with significant potential for various specialized applications.

Trifluoromethanesulfonic acid (TfOH), potassium hydroxide (KOH), calcium carbonate, n-heptane, tin octoate, 1-ethynyl-1-cyclohexanol, absolute alcohol (EtOH, ≥99.7 %), citric acid (CA, ≥99.5 %), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, ≥99 %), octamethylcyclotetrasiloxane (D4), urea, xylene, and anhydrous sodium sulfate were purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Dimethyldimethoxysilane (DMDMS), methylvinyldimethoxysilane (MVDMS), and vinyltrimethoxysilane (VTMS) were supplied by Hangzhou Jessica Chemical Co., Ltd. Vinyl silicone oil (RH-Vi302) was provided by Zhejiang RunHe Silicone New Material Co., Ltd. Divinyltetramethyldisiloxane (MMvi) was sourced from Zhejiang QuZhouJianCheng Silicone Co., Ltd. Methyl MQ resin (DY-MQ102N) was procured from Shandong Dayi Chemical Co., Ltd. β-(3, 4-epoxy-cyclohexyl) ethyltrimethoxysilane (A-186) was obtained from Momentive Silicone Co., Ltd. Hydrogen MMHQ resin (MQH9) was supplied by Ambia Special Silicone Resin (Nantong) Co., Ltd, and the platinum catalyst (CAT-PL-56) was acquired from Shin-Etsu Co., Ltd.

CA (0.58 g), urea (5.2 g), VTMS (2.4 g), APTES (3.58 g), H2O (10 mL), and EtOH (20 mL) were added to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and stirred until a homogeneous mixture was obtained. The autoclave was then sealed and heated in a thermostatically controlled oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the resulting R-CDs/PS composites were purified by centrifugation, first with distilled water and then with ethanol. Finally, the composites were dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 6 h to yield final R-CDs/PS composites.

DMDMS (50 g) and MVDMS (25 g) were added to a four-necked round-bottom flask equipped with a stirrer. The mixture was stirred at 40 °C for 10 min before a 0.5 wt% KOH solution (25 g) was added dropwise over 15 min using a constant-pressure funnel. After stirring for an additional 20 min, the mixture was heated successively to 52 and 72 °C for 2 h each. Once cooled, the mixture was washed with deionized water until the lower water layer became neutral. Hydroxyl vinyl silicone oil was obtained through filtration and distillation. Subsequently, A-186 (12.86 g) and tin octoate (0.02 g) were added to a four-necked round-bottom flask, and hydroxyl vinyl silicone oil (20.8 g) was added dropwise. The ensuing reaction took place at 50, 80, and 100 °C for 2 h each, and vinyl AD was obtained after cooling the mixture to room temperature.

FT-IR analysis of hydroxyl vinyl silicone oil (cm−1): Si-O-Si (900–1200), Si-O-CH3 (2811), Si-CH=CH2 (1597, 2961, 997) (Fig. S1a). FT-IR of vinyl AD (cm−1): Si-O-Si (900–1200), Si-O-CH3 (2813), Si-CH=CH2 (1602, 2989, 995), epoxy group (930) (Fig. S1b). 1H NMR of hydroxyl vinyl silicone oil (400 MHz, chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 0 (Si-CH3), 0.5 (CH2-CH), 1.5 (Si-OH), 3.5 (Si-O-CH3) and 5.5–6.0 (Si-CH=CH2) (Fig. S2). 1H NMR of vinyl AD (400 MHz, chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 0 (Si-CH3), 0.5 (CH2-CH), 1.0 (CH-CH3), 1.4 (CH2-CH2), 3.5 (Si-O-CH2CH3) and 5.5–6.0 (Si-CH=CH2) (Fig. S3). The refractive indices of hydroxyl vinyl silicone oil and vinyl AD are 1.416 and 1.429, respectively.

MMvi (20 g), methyl MQ resin (DY-MQ102N, 100 g), D4 (24 g), and xylene (80 g) were combined in a four-necked round-bottom flask equipped with a stirrer. TfOH (0.08 g) was then added, and the mixture was heated to 72 °C for 7 h. After cooling, the mixture was neutralized with calcium carbonate (1.6 g), washed, and distilled, yielding a colorless, transparent vinyl MDQ resin.

FT-IR analysis (cm−1): Si-O-Si (900–1200), Si-CH=CH2 (1594, 2956, 991) (Fig. S4). The UV–Vis transmission indicates that the light transmittance exceeds 95 % (Fig. S5). 29Si NMR (600 MHz, chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 11 (trimethyl-substituted silicon), 0 (dimethylvinyl-substituted silicon), 20 (silicon in (Me2SiO2/2)) and −110 (silicon in (SiO4/2)) (Fig. S6). 1H NMR (400 MHz, chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 0 (Si-CH3), 1.5 (Si-OH), 3.5 (Si-O-CH3) and 5.5–6.0 (Si-CH=CH2) (Fig. S7). The refractive index was measured to be 1.419.

The preparation process involved components A and B. In A, vinyl MDQ resin (4.20 g), RH-Vi302 (5.73 g), vinyl AD (0.30 g), and CA-PL-56 (0.015 g) were mixed and stirred uniformly. B comprised vinyl MDQ resin (4.20 g), RH-Vi302 (4.52 g), MQH9 (1.50 g), and 1-ethynyl-1-cyclohexanol (0.02 g), which were also mixed and stirred uniformly. A and B were then combined in a 1:1 wt ratio and homogenized. The curing process consisted of heating at 90 °C for 2 h followed by 150 °C for an additional 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of yellow and white LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: R-CDs/PS composites were mixed with SRAs at mass ratios of 7:100 for white LEDs and 10:100 for yellow LEDs. The mixtures were thoroughly stirred, dispensed onto 450 nm GaN chips, and cured at 90 °C for 2 h followed by 150 °C for another 2 h to produce the respective LEDs.

Fluorescence (PL) emission spectra and quantum yield (PLQY) were measured using a Pico-quant fluorescence spectrophotometer (FT300, Germany). UV–Vis absorption and transmission spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetime was determined using an LP920 instrument (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The size distribution of CDs was determined using a high-resolution field emission transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI, Czech Republic), operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Thermo Scientific NICOLET iS50 spectrometer. 1H NMR spectra were recorded with a Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer, while 2⁹Si NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker Avance 600 MHz spectrometer. Morphology and dimensions were analyzed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss Sigma 500, GRE) at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV, while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA). Crystallographic data were acquired via X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). Thermal behavior was investigated using an SDT-Q600 instrument (TA Instruments, USA), heating samples at 10 °C/min from 30 to 200 °C. Hydrophobicity was measured with a Harke-SPCA contact angle tester (Beijing, China). Adhesion tests were conducted using a push-pull machine (MFM1200, Rui Yin Co., Ltd., Hong Kong), while mechanical properties were measured with a universal material testing machine (Suzhou Tuobo Machinery Equipment Co., Ltd.). Optical characterization of the LEDs was performed with a Hopoocolor HPCS-320D Spectral Illuminometer.

Current commercial white LEDs are typically manufactured using Ce:YAG as the fluorescent material, where Ce:YAG is physically dispersed in an encapsulating material as a color conversion layer. However, this method may negatively impact the luminescence and stability of the LEDs. In contrast, our novel approach employs R-CDs/PS composites combined with cross-linked, highly adhesive SRAs onto a 450 nm GaN LED chip (Fig. 1 ) [34,35]. R-CDs/PS composites, which feature reactive vinyl groups, were synthesized via a one-step solvothermal process using CA, urea, APTES, and VTMS [36]. APTES, with an -NH2 group, can react with -COOH groups on the CDs surfaces to improve their solubility, dispersion, and to provide reactive sites. APTES also interacts with defect sites to enhance the photoluminescent properties and quantum yield of CDs [37]. VTMS is chosen for its vinyl group, which crosslinks with encapsulating materials to boost the mechanical and chemical stability of the powder. Thus, APTES and VTMS will improve the optical properties and durability of CDs composites for LED applications. To further improve encapsulation, vinyl AD and vinyl MDQ resins were designed and utilized as encapsulating materials. White LEDs were then fabricated by combining R-CDs/PS composites with vinyl AD, vinyl MDQ resin, MMHQ, and vinyl silicone oil. This mixture underwent a chemical cross-linking hydrosilylation reaction, catalyzed by a platinum catalyst, which significantly improved the LEDs' stability and extended their operational lifespan.

The optical properties of R-CDs/PS composites are shown in Fig. 2 . Significant absorption peaks were observed at 271, 350, and 450 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗ transition of C=C bonds and the n-π∗ transitions of C=O and C=N bonds, respectively (Fig. 2a) [38]. The composites exhibited optimal excitation wavelengths at 399 nm and 466 nm, with an emission peak at 528 nm. Visually, they appeared dark yellow under visible light and yellow under 365 nm UV light. As a contrast, the CDs prepared without addition of APTES and VTMS show no luminescence under ultraviolet light (Fig. S8), indicating that the CDs are encapsulated and fixed within the Si-O-Si network of polysiloxane matrices, reducing contact and inhibiting π-π stacking. This encapsulation overcomes the aggregation quenching effect of the CDs in the R-CDs/PS composite [39]. As shown in Fig. 2b, the photoluminescence (PL) emission peaks exhibited a slight redshift as the excitation wavelength varied from 360 nm to 495 nm. The absolute PLQY of R-CDs/PS composites was measured at 14.0 % (Fig. S9). The PL lifetime analysis using bi-exponential fitting indicated a lifetime of 17.90 ns (Fig. S10), confirming singlet state emission properties [40,41]. The stability of the R-CDs/PS composites was also measured. TGA results indicate that the R-CDs/PS composite retains 93.91 % of its solid content at 200 °C, with a weight loss of approximately 6 %, suggesting good thermal stability due to its polysiloxane network structure (Fig. S11). The fluorescence stability of the R-CD/PS composite was examined after being held for 2 h at various temperatures and after different UV exposure times. Fig. S12a shows slightly peak height changes in the PL spectra across 30–110 °C. At 130 °C, 150 °C, 170 °C, and 190 °C, fluorescence intensity retention rates were 84 %, 77 %, 60 %, and 52 %, respectively, indicating good thermal stability. Additionally, Fig. S12b demonstrates tiny variation in fluorescence intensity after 24–120 h of UV irradiation, indicating UV aging resistance. PL measurements at 30 °C, 50 °C, 70 °C, 90 °C, and 110 °C (Fig. S13) show that increasing temperature weakens the fluorescence intensity of the R-CD/PS composite due to accelerated non-radiative relaxation, leading to greater energy dissipation. However, the fluorescence intensity remains above 62.38 % at 110 °C compared to that at 30 °C, and the peak positions of the spectra remain unchanged, indicating that temperature affects the intensity rather than the energy level structure. These composites exhibited strong solid-state fluorescence and good stability, highlighting their potential for white LED applications.

The large particle size of luminescent materials can cause stratification and sedimentation within the encapsulating material, leading to reduced light efficiency and stability in LEDs. To address this, the morphology of R-CDs/PS composites was analyzed using FESEM and HRTEM. Fig. 3 a shows that R-CDs/PS composites have a particle size of around 150 nm, significantly smaller than conventional Ce:YAG, which is typically in the micron range. HRTEM further revealed spherical CDs within the composites. The encapsulation of CDs in silicone spheres made direct observation challenging, but statistical analysis of multiple HRTEM images determined an average particle size of approximately 2 nm (Fig. 3b) [42]. The composite edges were clearly delineated. Additionally, the XRD pattern (Fig. S14) confirmed that R-CDs/PS composites primarily consist of amorphous polysiloxanes, as indicated by the broad peak at 2θ = 22.2° [43].

The chemical composition and structure of R-CDs/PS composites were analyzed using FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 4 a) displayed characteristic peaks for -OH and NH2 (3100-3700 cm−1), Si-CH=CH2 (1606, 2939, 1040 cm−1), Si-O-Si and Si-O-C (900-1200 cm−1), and C=O (1600-1700 cm−1) [44,45]. The presence of C=O, N-H, and C-H bonds indicated the formation of -CONH, suggesting that R-CDs were embedded in the PS matrix via covalent bonding through amide linkages between carboxyl and amino groups [46]. The XPS spectrum revealed nitrogen (N), carbon (C), oxygen (O), and silicon (Si) elements in the composites (Fig. S15). The N 1s peak at 398.1 eV corresponded to N-H, while the peak at 400.2 eV indicated N-C bonds (Fig. 4b) [47]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) showed three peaks at 283.4 eV, 284.8 eV, and 287.2 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-N, and C=O, respectively [48]. In the Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 4d), Si-C and Si-O peaks appeared at 100.9 eV and 101.7 eV [49]. The XPS results were consistent with the FT-IR analysis, confirming the chemical structure. The embedding of CDs within the "Si-O-Si" network contributed to excellent solid-state fluorescence [50]. Additionally, the reactive vinyl groups in R-CDs/PS composites facilitate the formation of a stable color conversion layer for white LEDs by reacting with the encapsulating material.

To develop an encapsulating material capable of reacting with R-CDs/PS composites, vinyl-group-containing AD and MDQ resins were specifically designed and synthesized. Vinyl AD and MDQ resin offer strong adhesion, heat resistance, transparency, and durability, meeting LED packaging needs and extending lifespan. AD enhances adhesion to substrates like silver (Ag) and polyphthalamide (PPA), while MDQ resin provides mechanical properties and thermal stability [51,52]. The use of AD and MDQ resins as encapsulating materials was validated through FT-IR and NMR analyses (refer to Materials and Methods 2.3 and 2.4) [53,54]. The synthetic route for vinyl AD is illustrated in Fig. 5 a. First, hydroxyl vinyl silicone oil was produced via the hydrolysis and condensation of DMDMS and MVDMS. Vinyl AD was then synthesized through the condensation of hydroxyl vinyl silicone oil and A-186. AD contains vinyl groups for subsequent reactions and methoxyl groups to enhance adhesion properties at interfaces.The preparation of vinyl MDQ resin is shown in Fig. 5b. Starting with methyl MQ resin, TfOH catalyzed the exchange reaction of the M-unit ((CH3)3SiO1/2) with Vi-dimer. Vinyl MDQ resin was then synthesized via ring-opening rearrangement of D4. Compared to traditional silicone resin synthesis methods, this approach offered simplicity and control, allowing one-step production of vinyl MDQ resins under mild conditions with minimal production of low-boiling substances and increased efficiency [55,56].

The mechanical properties of SRAs, containing vinyl AD, vinyl MDQ resin, MMHQ, vinyl silicone oil, and a platinum catalyst, were further investigated. The ratio of component A (vinyl AD) to component B (vinyl MDQ resin) was kept constant. LED encapsulating SRAs with varying amounts of vinyl MDQ resin were cured at 90 °C for 2 h and 150 °C for 2 h, as outlined in Table S1. The tensile strength and elongation at break of SRAs varied with increasing vinyl MDQ resin content. The maximum tensile strength of 5.17 MPa, along with an elongation at break of 130.05 %, was achieved at a vinyl MDQ resin content of 41 % (Fig. 6 a). Sample 4 was selected as the optimal choice after a comprehensive assessment, as it demonstrated the highest tensile strength. Although sample 3 exhibited comparable tensile strength with a higher elongation at break, sample 4 outperformed in other critical aspects relevant to our specific application or research requirements. The elongation at break was measured to provide further insight into the material's mechanical properties, as excessive elongation can lead to over-deformation, potentially compromising the stability of encapsulated components. Stress-strain curves (Fig. 6b) confirmed optimal performance at this specific resin content level [57]. To enhance SRAs strength, the influence of methyl MQ resin on vinyl MDQ resin properties was also explored. Composed of monofunctional M ((CH3)3SiO1/2) and tetrafunctional Q (SiO2) units, the M/Q ratio affects polymerization, viscosity, crosslink density, and mechanical strength. Testing various M/Q ratios in vinyl MDQ resin synthesis show that an M/Q ratio of 0.8 yields the best mechanical properties in SRAs (Table S2).

To prevent moisture ingress, which can affect the lifespan of white LEDs, it is essential to bond SRAs to both the polyphthalamide resin (PPA) on the LED chip and the silver (Ag) metal on the underside. The bonding properties of the encapsulating material were studied under the same curing conditions, maintaining vinyl MDQ resin content at 41 %. The effect of Ag-PPA bonding was tested with varying vinyl AD ratios (Table S3). Shear strength peaked at 4 MPa with 1.5 % vinyl AD but slightly decreased as vinyl AD content increased, as shown in Fig. 7 a. This decline is likely due to the use of stannous octoate as a catalyst in vinyl AD synthesis, where excessive adhesive inhibits curing [58]. Fig. 7b 1–2 show the results of the red ink penetration test (red ink mixed with anhydrous ethanol in a 1:1 ratio, conducted at 80 °C for 1 h). The graph on the left represents the sample with vinyl AD, while the one on the right shows the sample without vinyl AD. The results demonstrate that adding 1.5 % vinyl AD improved Ag-PPA adhesion, prevented water vapor and red ink penetration, and ensured effective bonding and sealing. DSC analysis of SRAs (comprising 41 % vinyl MDQ resin and 1.5 % vinyl AD) indicated a reaction onset at 80.71 °C, completion at 135.4 °C, and a peak at 113 °C, as illustrated in Fig. S16. This gradual process effectively reduced stress and protected the chip [59].

After determining the optimal composition of vinyl MDQ resin (41 %) and vinyl AD (1.5 %), the bonding properties of SRAs with varying contents of R-CDs/PS composites on substrates such as Ag-PPA, Ag-Ag, and Al-Al were tested. The shear strength initially increased, then decreased, as shown in Table S4 and Fig. 7c [60]. This behavior can be attributed to an initial reinforcing effect followed by curing inhibition, likely due to amino groups on the surface of R-CDs/PS composites. At a 7:100 ratio of R-CDs/PS composites to SRAs, the interfacial diagram after bond failure indicated optimal performance [61]. Residual cured material at the interface, as shown in Fig. 7d 1–3, indicated cohesion failure, highlighting strong bonding performance. Additionally, Fig. S17a shows the water contact angle of SRAs with different R-CDs/PS composite fractions, all exceeding 105° (Table S5), matching reported PDMS resin compositions and demonstrating excellent hydrophobicity [62]. To improve device longevity, operational efficiency is maintained through effective heat dissipation and reduced thermal resistance, achieved by the dispersion of R-CDs/PS composites within SRAs (Fig. S17b). The results showed that thermal conductivity increased proportionally with R-CDs/PS composite content [63].

The optical properties and structure of fluorescent layers formed by cured SRAs with varying fractions of R-CDs/PS composites were further examined. Fig. 8 a shows photographs of these layers under visible light (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). The layers appeared uniform under visible light when R-CDs/PS composite ratio ranged from 0:100 to 10:100. As the concentration of R-CDs/PS composites increased, the layers transitioned from transparent to translucent, with a corresponding decrease in transparency (Fig. 8b). Under 365 nm UV light, the layers emitted a bright yellow-green fluorescence [64]. The transmittance of the layer-based LEDs for ratios from 0:100 to 10:100 (R-CDs/PS) was 93 %, 86 %, 82 %, 69 %, 66 %, and 56 %, respectively. PL intensity and absorption increased with higher proportions of R-CDs/PS composites, indicating effective dispersion within the SRAs due to their excellent compatibility (Fig. 8c–d). The PL peaks for the layers were at 511, 518, 521, 522, and 522 nm, while UV–vis absorption peaks were at 279, 283, 288, 289, and 291 nm. Importantly, FT-IR spectra showed similarity across the layers, with the absence of the characteristic vinyl peak at 1600 cm−1 after curing, confirming full reaction and consumption of vinyl groups during the curing process (Fig. 8e). This suggests the successful integration of R-CDs/PS composites into the encapsulating material.

SRAs encapsulating R-CDs/PS composites demonstrated excellent hydrophobicity, thermal conductivity, and adhesion properties, making them ideal for LED construction. The mixtures of R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs were stirred and deposited onto 450 nm GaN chips, followed by curing at 90 °C for 2 h and 150 °C for another 2 h to fabricate LEDs of various colors. Emission spectrum analysis revealed dominant wavelengths of 455, 459, 473, 569, and 580 nm, corresponding to dark blue, blue, light blue, white, and yellow LEDs, respectively. These LEDs were prepared using R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs in ratios of 1:100, 2:100, 5:100, 7:100, and 10:100 (Figures S18a-c, 9a-b). Specifically, the yellow LEDs were fabricated with a 10:100 ratio of R-CDs/PS composites to SRAs. Fig. 9 c indicated that the CIE of the LEDs with different proportions of R-CDs/PS composites content are (0.16, 0.05), (0.18, 0.09), (0.27, 0.26), (0.33, 0.34), and (0.50, 0.47), respectively. Traditional Ce:YAG phosphor-based white LEDs (Ce:YAG and SRAs at a 10:100 ratio) had a CIE coordinate of (0.33, 0.34), a CRI of 71.6, and a CCT of 5623 K (Fig. S18d). In comparison, the white LEDs prepared with R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs at a 7:100 ratio achieved a CIE of (0.33, 0.34), a CRI of 82.2, and a CCT of 5355 K, which is comparable to some other reported white LED prepared by different CDs-based PS composites (Table S6). Although the emission peak of the R-CDs/PS composite is only 528 nm, blue-shifted than that of Ce:YAG, it provides a broader wavelength range while enhancing light intensity in the blue-green and red regions. The proportion of red light significantly increases, and the color rendering index (R9) is 25 (Table S7), resulting in a higher CRI. Notably, the higher density of Ce:YAG phosphor particles compared to SRAs may cause settling during the curing process due to gravitational forces. As a result, commercial LEDs using Ce:YAG phosphors often require anti-settling agents to prevent this issue [65]. CIE values, which indicate luminous color positions, are crucial for assessing optical performance. CIE changes before and after curing can signal potential issues such as phosphor settlement or uneven distribution. The change of ΔY in the Y color coordinate before and after curing represents phosphor anti-settling performance. Tests show that the R-CDs/PS composite has a ΔY below 0.0012, which is much smaller than that of Ce:YAG (around 0.012), demonstrating R-CDs/PS composite has better anti-settling properties than micron-sized Ce:YAG particles (Fig. 9d–Table S8, and Table S9). The consistent luminous efficiency of white LEDs prepared with R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs suggests that the uniform particle sizes of the composites promoted even dispersion before curing. After curing, cross-linking reactions with SRAs further prevented sedimentation. Subsequently, these composites engaged in cross-linking reactions with SRAs after curing, effectively preventing sedimentation [66,67]. Furthermore, Fig. 9e shows the CIE of the white LEDs remained stable, shifting only slightly from (0.33, 0.34) to (0.33, 0.33) as the current increased from 20 to 100 mA, indicating stability across different driving currents. The operational stability of these white LEDs was also confirmed; they maintained emission intensity even after 7 days of continuous operation at 20 mA (Fig. 9f). These findings underscore the exceptional optical and operational stability of the white LEDs, highlighting their potential for practical applications.

In conclusion, this study successfully synthesized R-CDs/PS composites containing reactive vinyl groups as a color conversion material, along with SRAs incorporating vinyl AD and vinyl MDQ resin as encapsulating materials. R-CDs/PS composites exhibit excellent solid-state fluorescence properties and possess nano-scale characteristics. Additionally, SRAs demonstrate impressive mechanical properties and bonding strength. Notably, both R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs employ a chemical cross-linking hydrosilylation reaction via vinyl groups, forming a stable structure that effectively prevents stratification and sedimentation. The white LEDs produced by combining R-CDs/PS composites and SRAs in a 7:100 ratio exhibit desirable color characteristics, with a CIE of (0.33, 0.34), a CRI of 82.2, and a CCT of 5355 K, making them suitable for indoor lighting. Moreover, these white LEDs display excellent stability and reliability under various driving current conditions and extended operational scenarios. This study not only introduces a novel approach for synthesizing R-CDs/PS composites with reactive solid-state fluorescence but also offers an effective strategy for constructing stable white LEDs. Future research could enhance the PLQY and emission diversity of R-CDs/PS composites to expand their applications in white LEDs, achieving better light efficiency, color rendering, and luminous stability.

Liya Zhang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wei Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Peng Jin: Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhengguang Sun: Software. Yuan Zhan: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Bingbing Jiang: Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Our work is financially supported by the Foundation of Three Gorges Laboratory (SK232014)
